era_context: The early 15th century is marked by the late Medieval period in Europe, the Ming Dynasty's consolidation under the Yongle Emperor, the Ottoman Empire's recovery from the Interregnum, and the Hussite movement's emergence in Bohemia. The Renaissance is beginning in Italy; major construction projects are underway in Beijing. In Africa, the Songhai Empire is consolidating; in the Americas, the Aztec Empire is expanding.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most of Europe outside major political centers

events.title: Battle of Grunwald (Tannenberg) aftermath and Polish-Lithuanian consolidation
events.description: The Battle of Grunwald (also called Tannenberg) occurred in 1410, a major victory of the Polish-Lithuanian alliance over the Teutonic Knights. The battle marked a turning point in the conflict between the Teutonic Order and the Polish-Lithuanian state. The aftermath in 1410 saw the consolidation of Polish-Lithuanian power and the beginning of the decline of the Teutonic Order's dominance in the Baltic region. The battle had long-term consequences for the political structure of Eastern Europe.
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Grunwald is dated to 15 July 1410 and is well-documented in contemporary Polish, Lithuanian, and Teutonic sources.

events.title: Forbidden City construction progresses in Beijing
events.description: The Forbidden City construction project continues under the Yongle Emperor. Work began in 1406 and will continue until 1420. By 1410, the project is in its mid-phase, with major structural elements being erected. The construction involves hundreds of thousands of workers and represents one of the largest architectural undertakings of the Ming Dynasty. The project demonstrates Ming technological capabilities and the centralization of imperial power.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Official Ming Dynasty Records, contemporary)

events.certainty_note: The construction period (1406–1420) is well-documented; 1410 falls within this confirmed period.

events.title: Zheng He's first treasure voyage returns to China
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's first treasure voyage returns to China in 1410 (or 1411, sources vary). The voyage, which departed in 1405, visited Southeast Asia, India, Arabia, and East Africa. The fleet comprised over 300 ships and carried up to 28,000 personnel. The voyage demonstrated Ming naval supremacy and diplomatic reach. Zheng He will undertake six more voyages between 1407 and 1433, establishing Ming presence in the Indian Ocean and beyond.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Official Ming Dynasty Records, contemporary)
Ma Huan, Yingya Shenglan (Record of the Marvellous Sights of the Starry Raft, written 1416, eyewitness account)

events.certainty_note: The return date of Zheng He's first voyage is debated; sources place it between 1410 and 1411. The voyage itself is confirmed by multiple contemporary records.

events.title: Jan Hus active in Prague as reformer
events.description: Jan Hus, a Bohemian reformer and theologian, is active in Prague in 1410. Hus is a professor at Charles University and a preacher at the Bethlehem Chapel. He is advocating for Church reform, including communion in both kinds for laypeople and criticism of papal corruption. His teachings are gaining support among the Bohemian nobility and common people. Hus will be condemned at the Council of Constance in 1415 and executed in 1415, sparking the Hussite Wars.
events.certainty_note: Jan Hus's activity in Prague in 1410 is confirmed by his writings and later biographical accounts. He was a prominent figure in Prague's religious and intellectual life.

events.title: Portuguese exploration of the African coast continues
events.description: Portuguese explorers, sponsored by Prince Henry the Navigator, continue systematic exploration of the West African coast. By 1410, Portuguese ships have established trading posts and are gathering information on African geography, peoples, and resources. The exploration is motivated by trade, religious conversion, and geographic curiosity. These voyages lay the groundwork for the Portuguese slave trade and colonial expansion.

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery of Guinea (written 1450s, based on earlier records)

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration was ongoing; specific events in 1410 are not well-documented. The broader campaign is confirmed, but individual voyage dates are often approximate.
cross_references: 1488-001 (Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope)

disconfirming_evidence: The return date of Zheng He's first voyage is disputed; some sources place it in 1411 rather than 1410. The claim that Portuguese exploration was systematic and continuous is not well-supported by contemporary documentation; most detailed records come from later compilations (Zurara, 1450s). The Battle of Grunwald's long-term significance is sometimes overstated; while it was a major victory, the Teutonic Order remained a significant power in the Baltic for decades afterward.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1410 is sparse but somewhat better than earlier years. The Battle of Grunwald is well-documented in contemporary Polish, Lithuanian, and Teutonic sources. Chinese events are documented in official Ming records. African, American, and Oceanic events are entirely undocumented. The historical record is heavily biased toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are largely invisible.

graph_edges.note: Battle of Grunwald in 1410 led to peace negotiations beginning in 1411
First voyage's return in 1410 was part of the larger series of Zheng He's treasure voyages
Jan Hus's reformist activity in 1410 led to his execution in 1415
